hook: A poor scribe carving clay tablets became the richest man in the greatest city of the ancient world, using rules so simple a child could grasp them. The only thing standing between you and the same result is the quiet discipline to actually live by them.

sections.h: Two Broke Men and a Scribe
p: Bansir the chariot builder and Kobbi the lyre player were among the most skilled craftsmen in Babylon, and both were completely broke. They had worked hard their whole lives and had nothing to show for it, and one day they finally stopped to ask the question most people never say out loud. Why do we labor year after year and still our purses stay empty, while other men no smarter than us grow rich?

They made one wise decision. Instead of trading complaints with other poor men, they went to ask someone who had actually solved the problem. Their old friend Arkad was famous as the richest man in Babylon, yet he had started exactly where they were, a humble scribe carving words into clay for almost nothing. He inherited no fortune and caught no lucky break. He simply learned the laws that move wealth toward a person, and those laws, Clason insists, have not changed in four thousand years.

sections.h: A Part of All You Earn
p: Arkad's secret came from a rich money lender named Algamish, who gave him the single sentence the whole book is built on. I found the road to wealth when I decided that a part of all I earned was mine to keep. Arkad was confused, because surely everything he earned was already his. Algamish laughed and named the trap almost everyone lives inside. You pay the sandal maker, the food seller, the landlord, everyone but yourself, and at month's end you have kept nothing.

The cure is to reverse the order. When money comes in, the very first payment you make, before rent, before food, before any bill, is to yourself. Take at least one tenth off the top, put it away, and live on the nine tenths that remain. Most people do it backwards, saving whatever happens to be left at the end, and the cruel truth is that nothing is ever left. There is always one more thing to buy.
sections.pull: A part of all you earn is yours to keep, so pay yourself first, before you pay anyone else.

sections.h: Seven Cures for a Lean Purse
p: When the King of Babylon saw that wealth pooled in a few hands, he asked Arkad to teach the whole city, and the lessons came down to us as the Seven Cures for a Lean Purse. The first is to start thy purse to fattening, the tenth made into law. For every ten coins you earn, spend only nine. The second is to control thy expenditures, and here Arkad names the quiet enemy of every budget. What we call our necessary expenses will always grow to equal our income unless we protest to the contrary.

So you must separate true needs from mere desires, which are endless and always disguise themselves as needs. The third cure is to make thy gold multiply by putting savings to work, and then putting the earnings to work too, so the children of your gold and their children all labor for you. That is the ancient name for compounding. The fourth cure is to guard thy treasures from loss. Before you chase what an investment might earn, ask the harder question of whether you can get your principal back at all.

The last three round it out. Make of thy dwelling a profitable investment, directing money toward something you own rather than renting forever into a landlord's pocket. Insure a future income, setting money aside now for old age and for your family in case you are gone. And increase thy ability to earn, because sharpening your own skill is the one asset no thief can steal and no bad market can destroy.
sections.pull: What we call our necessary expenses will always grow to equal our income unless we protest to the contrary.

p: Arkad taught the same wisdom a second time through his son Nomasir, whom he sent into the world for ten years with a bag of gold and a clay tablet carved with the Five Laws of Gold. Nomasir ignored the tablet at first, bet on a horse race, trusted a swindler, and lost every coin. Only when he was destitute did he finally study the laws, live by them, and return home ten years later far richer than the gold his father had given him.

The laws sharpen the cures from a new angle. Gold comes gladly to the one who saves at least a tenth. It labors and multiplies for the owner who finds it profitable employment. It clings to the cautious owner who takes counsel from people wise in handling money. It slips away from the man who invests in what he does not understand. And it flees the man who chases impossible returns or believes the smooth talk of tricksters and schemers.

Arkad learned the last laws the hard way. Early on he handed his first savings to a brickmaker to buy jewels, and the brickmaker, who knew bricks and nothing of gems, was cheated with worthless glass. Would you ask the baker about the stars? Take financial advice only from those genuinely skilled in the exact matter at hand, and put your gold only into what you can clearly understand.

The most powerful story in the book belongs to Dabasir, a camel trader who spent freely, borrowed to cover it, and let his debts pile up until they crushed him. He fled his creditors, fell in with robbers, and was sold into slavery, a free tradesman reduced to a slave by runaway debt. At his lowest, a woman asked him whether his soul was that of a free man or a slave, and something in him woke up. He escaped across the desert and returned to Babylon with one purpose, to face every creditor and repay every coin.

His plan is the practical heart of the book. He wrote down every debt in full, hiding from no part of the total, and then split his income three ways. He would live on seventy percent of what he earned, hand twenty percent to his creditors divided fairly among them, and keep the last ten percent for himself, so he was still building wealth even while climbing out of the hole. Most of his creditors were glad, because a steady honest payment beats a debtor who vanishes.

Coin by coin, month by month, Dabasir cleared every debt, restored his honor, and grew wealthy again, all on the same trade and the same city he already had. What changed was not his income. It was his resolve.
sections.pull: Where the determination is, the way can be found.

p: Two final lessons put a floor under all the arithmetic. The first is about luck. The men of Babylon debated whether good fortune comes from the gaming tables or the gods, and concluded it comes from neither. Luck follows action. When a real opportunity appears, the lucky man acts on it promptly, while the unlucky man delays, plans to get to it tomorrow, and lets it slip to someone else. Clason names the villain plainly. Procrastination is the thief of opportunity.

The second is protection. Babylon survived every siege because of its great wall, and Clason turns that wall into a picture of the reserves that stand between you and ruin. An illness, a lost job, a death in the family, these blows come to everyone, and the person with no wall is destroyed by the first one that lands. Build your walls, your savings and your insurance, before the day you need them, because that day is not the day you can build them.

Underneath it all sits the simplest truth of the book, told through a slave who became a merchant prince. Work, embraced gladly instead of resented, is the finest friend a person can have. The tenths and the laws and the compounding all rest on the willingness to do honest work, and to make that work finally pay you by keeping a part of everything it earns.

chart.title: Dabasir's plan for escaping debt
chart.subtitle: How the camel trader split every coin he earned while repaying
chart.caption: Illustrative. Dabasir divided all he earned three ways, living on seventy, repaying creditors twenty, and keeping ten for himself so he still built wealth while clearing the debt.

takeaways: Set up an automatic transfer of at least ten percent of every paycheck the moment it arrives, before you have a chance to spend it.
Write down where your money actually went last month, then find one desire that is posing as a need and cut it.
If you carry debt, list every debt in full and build Dabasir's plan: live on seventy percent, pay creditors twenty, keep ten for yourself.
Put your savings to work and reinvest the earnings, so the interest itself starts earning interest.
Before any investment, ask whether you can get your principal back, and take advice only from people truly skilled in that field.
Pick one skill that would raise your earning power and take the first concrete step on it this week.
Act on one sound opportunity you have been putting off, before procrastination steals it for good.
